text: The surgical team performs a left hepatectomy for a bleeding hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC). A right subcostal incision with midline extension is visible. The Makuchi incision is opened, revealing active bleeding from the tumor and old clots in the surgical field.
The surgeon ligates the cystic duct after previously ligating the cystic artery, and proceeds to remove the gallbladder from its position.

The surgical field shows continuous bleeding from the liver tumor, with blood collecting in the area. The surgeon attempts to mobilize the hepatic artery, preparing to ligate it along with the portal vein to control the bleeding.

The surgeon identifies the left hepatic artery, which is ligated, clipped, and divided. Despite this, there is persistent bleeding visible in the surgical field. The dissection continues around the porta hepatis to identify the right hepatic artery and confirm the artery to segment four before ligation. The portal vein is looped for further control.

The surgeon ligates and divides the middle hepatic artery, also known as the artery to segment four. The portal vein is mobilized, with its tributary to segment four clipped. The left branch of the portal vein is looped using a blue sling, while the right branch is clearly identified. The portal vein is double ligated and divided, and the remnant end of the left portal vein branch is oversewn with a continuous proline stitch.

The surgeon removes the clamp to check for active bleeding before cutting the proline suture. The surgical field appears dry, indicating successful ligation of the vessel inflow with no active bleeding present.

The surgeon divides the left triangular ligament and mobilizes the left lobe of the liver, marking the line for parenchymal transection. Two stay sutures are placed, and dissection begins with a harmonic scalpel. The vein to segment five is identified and followed towards the inferior vena cava (IVC), with the metaphylactic vein being defined along segment five.

The surgeon proceeds with parenchymal transection using harmonic devices, clips, sutures, and calies, while attempting to follow the middle hepatic vein towards the inferior vena cava (IVC). The surgical field shows the ongoing transection process.

The cut surface of the liver appears irregular and ragged due to the parenchymal division technique. The surgeon has ligated the segment IV branch and is proceeding along the middle hepatic vein. When bleeding occurs from the middle hepatic vein during parenchymal transection, the surgeon uses forceps and suction to dry the area, followed by a proline suture to control hemostasis. Additional tools like a multiplier or clips may be used to manage bleeding. As vessels are ligated and cut, more liver parenchyma is exposed.

The surgeon double clips, ligates, and divides the segment 4A branch leading towards the middle hepatic vein. A clamp is applied on the left hepatic vein as the parenchymal transduction is being completed.
The specimen from the left hepatectomy is displayed. A multiperforated romodrain is placed near the cut surface, and surgical glue is applied to control bleeding from the cut surface.
